cards.front: What is the role of a **router**?
cards.back: Forwards packets between IP networks (L3). Makes routing decisions based on dest IP + routing table. One broadcast domain per interface.

cards.front: What is the role of an **L2 switch**?
cards.back: Forwards frames within one broadcast domain based on dest MAC. Builds MAC table from src MAC. One collision domain per port.

cards.front: What is the role of an **L3 switch**?
cards.back: Combines L2 switching (within VLAN) and L3 routing (between VLANs/networks). Faster than separate router; common in distribution/core layer.

cards.front: What does a **firewall** do vs an **IPS**?
cards.back:
- **Firewall**: stateful filter; allow/deny based on src/dst/port/zone
- **IPS** (Intrusion Prevention System): inspects packet content for known attack signatures + behavior; blocks inline
Modern NGFW combines both.
Centralized brain for lightweight access points (LWAPP/CAPWAP). Pushes config, manages roaming, handles encryption, runs RRM for channel/power tuning.

cards.front: Difference between **Cisco DNA Center** and a **WLC**?
cards.back: DNA Center = SDN controller for entire enterprise campus (routers + switches + APs); intent-based, automation, assurance. WLC = wireless-only.
Power over Ethernet — sends DC power over Cat5e/6 cable to powered devices (APs, phones, cameras). Standards: 802.3af (15W), 802.3at PoE+ (30W), 802.3bt PoE++ (60-100W).

cards.front: **Two-tier** topology = [...]. Distribution and core layers merged into one. Used in smaller campus.

cards.back: **Core** (high-speed backbone) → **Distribution** (policy, routing between VLANs, redundancy) → **Access** (end-host connections).

cards.front: What's the **spine-leaf** topology and where is it used?
cards.back: Every leaf connects to every spine; no leaf-to-leaf or spine-to-spine links. Used in data centers for **east-west** traffic (server-to-server). Exactly 2 hops between any two leaves. CoreWeave EVI01 uses this.
Three-tier optimizes north-south (campus/enterprise); spine-leaf optimizes east-west (data center). Spine-leaf gives predictable latency: always 2 hops between leaves.
On-prem = company owns/operates physical infra in own DC. Cloud = compute/storage rented from provider (AWS/Azure/GCP/CW), accessed over WAN/internet.
Small Office / Home Office — single integrated device (router + switch + AP + firewall in one box). 1-50 users.

cards.front: **Single-mode fiber** vs **multimode fiber** — when use each?
cards.back:
- **Single-mode** (yellow jacket, 9μm core, laser): long-haul, low loss, kilometers. ~10G/40G/100G+
- **Multimode** (orange/aqua/violet, 50/62.5μm core, LED/VCSEL): short-distance, within building, up to ~400m. Cheaper optics.

cards.front: **Straight-through** vs **crossover** copper cable — when use each?
cards.back:
- **Straight-through**: connecting different device types (PC↔switch, switch↔router)
- **Crossover**: same device types (switch↔switch, router↔router, PC↔PC)
Modern devices use **Auto-MDIX** — figures it out automatically.
Two devices transmitting at the same time on a shared (half-duplex) segment. Increment `show interface` collision counter. Solution: full-duplex on both ends.

cards.front: What's a **duplex mismatch** and what symptom does it cause?
cards.back: One side full-duplex, other side half-duplex. Half-duplex side sees collisions; full-duplex side sees late collisions + runts + FCS errors. Throughput tanks. Always set duplex/speed explicitly OR let both sides auto-negotiate.

cards.front: How to check interface errors on Cisco IOS?
cards.back:
```
show interface <int>
```
Look for: input errors, CRC, frame, overrun, ignored, runts, giants, collisions, late collisions, output errors. Non-zero increasing = problem.

- **Runt** — frame smaller than 64 bytes (collision fragment or misconfig)
- **Giant** — frame larger than max MTU (default 1518 / 1522 with 802.1Q)
- **Baby giant** — slightly over (jumbo not enabled but tagged frame arrives)
